title: Kaitiakitanga in Action: Guardians of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku | Te Mahi Kaitiaki: Ngā Kaitiaki o Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku
subject: Social Studies
culturalContext: This lesson is deeply rooted in our local awa (river) - Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku. Students will explore the historical significance of this waterway as a traditional food gathering site for Ngāti Kahungunu, its current ecological challenges including pollution and sedimentation, and the ongoing restoration efforts led by local hapū. The context connects to the pūrākau (legend) of Hinekorako, the water maiden who gifted the springs that feed our awa.
learningObjectives: Identify and explain three traditional Māori practices of kaitiakitanga used to protect Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku
Analyze current environmental threats to the awa using water quality testing data and create a simple data visualization
Collaboratively design and present a practical kaitiakitanga action plan addressing one specific issue affecting our local awa
Reflect on personal connections to local waterways and commit to one ongoing guardianship action

activities.title: Waiata Welcome & Awa Connection
activities.description: Begin with a waiata that honors water (e.g., 'E rere e te wai') at the banks of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku. Students share their own experiences and whakapapa connections to local waterways through pepeha if comfortable. Introduce the pūrākau of Hinekorako.

activities.title: Awa Health Check: Field Investigation
activities.description: Using water testing kits, students measure pH, nitrate levels, turbidity, and macroinvertebrate diversity at three sites along the awa. They record observations of riparian health and human impacts. Data is compiled collaboratively.

activities.title: Kōrero with Kaitiaki: Community Expert Session
activities.description: A local kaumātua or representative from the Mangakōtukutuku Restoration Trust visits the class (or joins via video). They share traditional knowledge, historical significance, current challenges, and community efforts. Students prepare questions in advance.

activities.title: Action Plan Design: Rāhui to Restoration
activities.description: In small groups, students analyze their collected data and expert knowledge to design a specific kaitiakitanga action. Options include: creating educational signage for the riverbank, planning a native planting day, designing a community awareness campaign about pollution, or proposing a modern rāhui (temporary protection) protocol.

activities.title: Pepeha Presentation & Commitment
activities.description: Groups present their action plans to the class and invited community members. Each student ends by articulating their personal role as a kaitiaki, updating their pepeha to include their responsibility to Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku, and making a specific commitment.

resources: Mangakōtukutuku Restoration Trust: Local experts and potential guest speakers
NIWA's 'Stream Health Monitoring and Assessment Kit' (SHMAK) or similar water testing kits
'Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku: A Historical Perspective' - a resource held by the college library and local marae
Digital access to the Land Air Water Aotearoa (LAWA) website for comparing local water quality data

tasks: Field journal entries documenting water testing methods, results, and initial observations
Group kaitiakitanga action plan proposal (written or multimedia presentation)
Individual reflection on personal guardianship responsibilities and commitments

nzcAlignment: Social Studies L4: Understand how people participate individually and collectively in response to community challenges
Social Studies L4: Understand how the ways in which leadership of groups is acquired and exercised have consequences for communities and societies
Social Studies L5: Understand how people's management of resources impacts on environmental and social sustainability
Tikanga ā-Iwi: Te ao Māori - Te whakaritenga pāpori me te ahurea Māori (Māori social organization and culture)
